messages.content:
Lesson 2 — A Contribution to the Critique of Political Economy - Presentation26 Slides
The Twofold Character of the Commodity
  • At first sight the wealth of society under the capitalist system presents itself as an immense accumulation of commodities, its unit being a single commodity.
  • Every commodity has a twofold aspect, that of use value and exchange value.
  • A commodity is first of all any thing necessary, useful or pleasant in life, an object of human wants, a means of existence in the broadest sense of the word.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the text, what is exchange value nothing but?
questions.explanation: The text states directly that exchange value is nothing but mere labor-time contained in commodities.

questions.question: What does the text identify as the substance expressed in exchange value?
questions.explanation: The text asks us to consider the nature of labor which is expressed in exchange value and shows it must be uniform, homogeneous, simple labor.

questions.question: How does the text describe the relation between every form of production and legal relations?
options: Every form of production creates its own legal relations
Legal relations are accidental to production
Production and law have no connection
questions.explanation: The text states that every form of production creates its own legal relations, forms of government, etc.

questions.question: Why does gold become the measure of value according to the text?
options: Because all commodities measure their exchange values in gold in proportion to equal labor-time
Because it has intrinsic aesthetic value
questions.explanation: Gold becomes the measure of value because all commodities measure their exchange values in gold in proportion as equal quantities contain the same amount of labor-time.

questions.question: What does the text say price represents?
options: The form into which exchange value is converted when it appears in circulation
questions.explanation: Price is the form into which the exchange value of commodities is converted when it appears within the sphere of circulation.

cards.front: What is the twofold aspect of every commodity?
cards.back: Use value and exchange value.

cards.front: What does the text state exchange value is nothing but?
cards.back: Mere labor-time contained in commodities.

cards.front: What kind of labor creates exchange value?
cards.back: Uniform, homogeneous, simple labor.

cards.front: How does gold become the measure of value?
cards.back: Because commodities measure their exchange values in gold in proportion to equal labor-time.

cards.front: What does every form of production create according to the text?
cards.back: Its own legal relations, forms of government, etc.

cards.front: Where is use-value realized?
cards.back: Only in the process of consumption.

cards.front: What is price?
cards.back: The form into which exchange value is converted in circulation.

cards.front: What does the isolation of gold as money express?
cards.back: The disintegration of the metamorphosis of commodities into two separate acts.

cards.front: What does the text say production stands for in the connection of production, distribution, exchange and consumption?
cards.back: The general.

cards.front: What is gold as money in contrast to commodities?
cards.back: The bodily representative of material wealth.

slides.title: The Twofold Character of the Commodity
bullets: At first sight the wealth of society under the capitalist system presents itself as an immense accumulation of commodities, its unit being a single commodity.
Every commodity has a twofold aspect, that of use value and exchange value.
A commodity is first of all any thing necessary, useful or pleasant in life, an object of human wants, a means of existence in the broadest sense of the word.

This dual nature means that a thing like a coat or a loaf of bread must first answer a concrete human need before it can enter the market at all, yet once there its usefulness is eclipsed by the quantitative relation it bears to every other commodity, so that the very properties that make it desirable threaten to vanish behind the single imperative of exchange.
slides.notes: Opens Chapter I by establishing the dual nature that grounds the labour theory of value.

slides.title: Use-Value Realized Only in Consumption
bullets: Use-value has a value only in use and is realized only in the process of consumption.
The same use-value may be utilized in various ways, yet the extent of its possible applications is circumscribed by its distinct properties.
Use-value in this indifference to the nature of its economic destination lies outside the sphere of investigation of political economy.

Thus the economist must treat use-value as a mute precondition that drops away the moment exchange begins, leaving him to track only the quantities that survive in the market after the bread is eaten or the coat is worn.
slides.notes: Distinguishes material properties from the economic relations that form the base.

slides.title: Exchange-Value as a Quantitative Relation
bullets: Exchange-value appears at first sight as a quantitative relation, as a proportion in which use-values are exchanged for one another.
In such a relation they constitute equal exchangeable quantities.
The exchange value of a palace can be expressed in a certain number of boxes of shoe-blacking.

Yet this numerical proportion can only hold if some common element underlies both the palace and the shoe-blacking, allowing them to be treated as multiples of one another despite their utterly different qualities.
slides.notes: Shows how exchange value abstracts from concrete use.

slides.title: Commodities as Materialized Labor
bullets: Use-values are primarily means of existence and are themselves products of social life, the result of expended human vital power, materialized labor.
As the embodiment of social labor, all commodities are the crystallization of the same substance.
Let us now consider the nature of this substance, i.e., of labor, which is expressed in exchange value.

This reduction of varied human activities to a single measurable substance means that exchange value can equate entirely different products and labors, turning concrete work into an abstract social average whose fluctuations then govern the lives of producers behind their backs.
slides.notes: Introduces labour as the substance of value.

slides.title: Equal Exchange Values Contain Equal Labor
bullets: Let one ounce of gold, one ton of iron, one quarter of wheat and twenty yards of silk represent equal exchange values.
As equivalents, in which the qualitative difference between their use-values has been eliminated, they represent equal volumes of the same kind of labor.
The labor which is equally embodied in all of them must be uniform, homogeneous, simple labor.

This common measure therefore presupposes a society in which every concrete act of labor is already being reduced to an average standard by the compulsion of market competition, so that the very possibility of equal exchange rests on the prior social equalization of human activity itself.
slides.notes: Derives the necessity of abstract labour from equal exchange.

slides.title: Labor Indifferent to Its Concrete Form
bullets: It matters as little in the case of labor whether it be embodied in gold, iron, wheat, or silk, as it does in the case of oxygen, whether it appears in the rust of iron, in the atmosphere, in the juice of a grape, or in the blood of a human being.
Just as labor, which creates exchange value, is indifferent to the material of use-values, so it is to the special form of labor itself.

Because labor counts only as a quantity of time, any concrete skill or material can be swapped for another without altering the magnitude of value, so that the market treats the weaver and the miner as interchangeable units of the same social substance.

This reduction therefore compels workers to offer their capacities as abstract labor power, exposing them to constant relocation across industries and sharpening competition that presses wages toward the bare cost of keeping labor alive.
slides.notes: Explains why concrete labours are reduced to one homogeneous labour.

slides.title: Exchange Value Is Labor-Time
bullets: Exchange value is nothing but mere labor-time contained in commodities.
The different use-values are the products of the work of different individuals, consequently the result of various kinds of labor differing individually from one another.
Yet as exchange values they are equal volumes of the same kind of labor.

This abstraction therefore compels every producer to measure success solely by the hours extracted from living labor, turning the varied rhythms of concrete work into a uniform race against the clock that governs all market outcomes.
slides.notes: States the labour theory of value directly.

slides.title: Gold as the Direct Incarnation of Universal Labor-Time
bullets: Commodities which are use-values by nature acquire a form in which they appear in idea to each other as exchange values, as definite quantities of incorporated universal labor-time.
The first necessary step in this process is the setting apart by the commodities of a specific commodity, say gold, as the direct incarnation of universal labor-time, or the universal equivalent.

Once gold occupies this position every other commodity must translate its own hidden labor-time into a visible quantity of gold before exchange can occur, so that private producers discover only afterward whether their work counted as part of society’s total labor.

This arrangement therefore turns gold into the visible measure against which all concrete labors are judged, yet it also installs a permanent tension because any change in the labor required to produce gold immediately revalues every other commodity without a single producer having altered their own activity.
slides.notes: Shows how money emerges from the labour theory.

slides.title: Commodities Express Their Values in Gold
bullets: In the series of equations iron, wheat, coffee, potash, etc. appear to each other as embodiments of homogeneous labor, namely, as labor materialized in money.
From all the peculiarities of the different kinds of concrete labor represented in the different use-values are completely eliminated.

Once gold serves as this common measure, every useful article can be weighed against every other in a single scale of magnitude, so that the workshop and the market begin to speak the same quantitative language.

Yet the very act that renders labors interchangeable also conceals the living activity that created them, allowing the glittering metal to appear as the true source of worth while the worker remains invisible behind the equation.
slides.notes: Illustrates the reduction performed by exchange.

slides.title: Gold Becomes the Measure of Value
bullets: Gold becomes the measure of value, because all commodities measure their exchange values in gold, in proportion as a certain quantity of gold and a certain quantity of the commodity contain the same amount of labor-time.
It is only by virtue of this function of being a measure of value that gold becomes a universal equivalent or money.

This anchoring of all prices to gold therefore transmits every change in mining productivity straight into the exchange ratios of every other product, so the hidden conditions of extraction govern the visible world of commerce.

Money therefore emerges not as a neutral tool but as the concrete expression of social labor, compelling isolated producers to recognize one another through a single material standard that stands above them all.
slides.notes: Links measure of value to labour-time equality.

slides.title: Price as the Money Form of Exchange Value
bullets: The exchange value of commodities thus expressed in the form of a universal equivalent and, moreover, as a numerical proportion of this equivalent, in terms of one specific commodity, is price.
Price is the form into which the exchange value of commodities is converted when it appears within the sphere of circulation.

Once exchange value wears the mask of price it must be measured against one chosen money commodity, so every product now carries a visible number that stands for invisible hours of labor performed elsewhere in society.

Yet this same numerical form permits prices to drift above or below the labor time they represent, turning the quiet law of value into the noisy struggle of markets where unsold goods expose the gap between essence and appearance.
slides.notes: Defines price as the phenomenal form of labour value.

slides.title: Money as Unity of Measure and Medium
bullets: A commodity thus becomes money only in its combined capacity of a measure of value and medium of circulation.
The unity of the measure of value and medium of circulation is money.
Only when the same commodity serves both functions at once can the labour time crystallized in every product receive a uniform social expression that actually guides exchange.
Should these two aspects pull apart, prices detach from values and the circulation of commodities begins to generate gluts or shortages that no amount of individual labour can correct.
slides.notes: Shows money as the completed form arising from value relations.

slides.title: Gold as the Bodily Representative of Material Wealth
bullets: While commodities represent in their prices the universal equivalent or abstract wealth, viz., gold, the latter represents in its use-value the use-values of all commodities.
Gold is therefore the bodily representative of material wealth.

In practice this means the capitalist must chase actual gold bars and coins, not mere paper promises, because only the metal can be locked away as finished wealth that no further exchange can dissolve.

The drive therefore turns mining and conquest into permanent necessities of the system, since fresh gold must constantly be dug from the earth to keep the bodily representative from running short and exposing the limits of abstract price.
slides.notes: Contrasts abstract labour embodied in money with concrete wealth.

slides.title: Production Creates Its Own Legal Relations
bullets: Every form of production creates its own legal relations, forms of government, etc.
The crudity and the shortcomings of the conception lie in the tendency to see but an accidental reflective connection in what constitutes an organic union.

Thus the wage contract and the laws of property that enforce it grow straight out of the wage-labour relation itself, turning the daily sale of labour-power into a normal and apparently permanent feature of social life.

When the legal order is treated as a mere mirror of economics rather than its living product, the real movement of production is hidden and the struggle to transform both base and superstructure is disarmed in advance.
slides.notes: States the base-superstructure relation in Marx's own words.

slides.title: The Superstructure Arises from the Base
bullets: All production is appropriation of nature by the individual within and through a definite form of society.
When the social conditions corresponding to a certain stage of production are in a state of formation or disappearance, disturbances of production naturally arise.

These definite social forms of appropriation therefore crystallize into legal titles, political institutions and moral codes that present themselves as eternal yet remain tethered to the momentary balance of production relations.

When those relations enter the phase of formation or disappearance the inherited superstructure no longer secures the conditions of appropriation and instead becomes an active source of blockage and conflict.
slides.notes: Shows how legal and political forms rest on the economic base of production.

slides.title: Production Determines Distribution and Exchange
bullets: Production, distribution, exchange, and consumption form a perfect connection, production standing for the general, distribution and exchange for the special, and consumption for the individual.
Production is determined by universal natural laws, while distribution depends on social chance.

Because production rests on the concrete labor process and its tools, any shift in those forces immediately presses against inherited rules of sharing, forcing society either to adapt its customs or to suffer deepening crises of overproduction and want.

Distribution and exchange therefore function as the arena where the class relations forged in production are fought out, so that private ownership of factories and land converts the collective labor of many into the private wealth of a few.
slides.notes: Places production as the determining base.

slides.title: The Economic Structure as the Real Foundation
bullets: The so-called general conditions of all production are nothing but abstract conceptions which do not go to make up any real stage in the history of production.
Every form of production creates its own legal relations.

Because production always unfolds in definite historical forms, any legal framework that pretends to rest on universal principles soon collides with the concrete relations that actually govern work, ownership and exchange.

The result is a permanent tension: when the economic base shifts, inherited legal relations begin to fetter further development, forcing struggles over property, contracts and the distribution of the values labour has created.
slides.notes: Emphasizes that concrete production relations form the base.

slides.title: Labor as the Source of Exchange Value Alone
bullets: Labor as a source of use-value and labor as the creator of certain social form of wealth or exchange value must be distinguished.
The digging of gold, the extraction of iron from a mine, the raising of wheat and the weaving of silk are so many kinds of labor, differing in quality.

This distinction shows that only under commodity production does labor acquire the social power to create exchange value, so that useful objects become bearers of wealth solely because private efforts have been forced into equivalence through sale.

The qualitative differences among labors therefore cannot determine how much value each creates; instead the market reduces them all to a single homogeneous expenditure of human energy whose quantity alone decides the proportions in which products exchange, leaving the individual producer at the mercy of social averages he cannot control.
slides.notes: Clarifies that only abstract labour creates exchange value.

slides.title: Abstract Wealth Individualized in Money
bullets: Money satisfies every want since it can be directly converted into the object of any want.
In its virgin metallic state it holds locked up all the material wealth which lies unfolded in the world of commodities.

Because money can be exchanged for anything at any moment, the drive to accumulate it overrides the production of particular goods, turning wealth into a pure quantitative chase detached from human needs.

This hoarding of potential riches in metallic form therefore concentrates social power in the hands of those who hold money, while leaving the actual creators of wealth dependent on its owners for access to the means of life.
slides.notes: Shows how labour value takes the independent form of money.

slides.title: Hoarding as the Separation of Sale and Purchase
bullets: The independent isolation of gold as money is a material expression of the disintegration of the process of circulation, or of the metamorphosis of commodities, into two separate acts independent of each other.
In order that money should flow continuously as coin, coin must constantly coagulate as money.

When a seller refuses to re-enter the market as a buyer, the value created by labor remains frozen in coin and the next producer is left without the means to sell, so the social metabolism of commodities stalls.

Hoarding therefore turns the formal independence of sale and purchase into a real threat of general crisis, because the same act that preserves value in money form simultaneously withholds the demand that alone can validate that value.
slides.notes: Illustrates a consequence of the value form in circulation.

slides.title: The Circulation Process Requires Reserve Funds
bullets: The continuous flow of coin depends on its constant accumulations in the form of reserve-funds of coin which spring up throughout the sphere of circulation and form sources of supply.
The formation, distribution, disappearance, and reformation of these reserve funds is constantly changing.

These hoards must sit idle at countless points along the circuit, binding a definite quantity of social wealth that cannot be used for fresh production and thereby exerting constant downward pressure on the overall rate of accumulation.
When the reserves shrink too far or pile up in the wrong hands, the chain of payments snaps, commodities remain unsold, and the very continuity that value requires is thrown into crisis.
slides.notes: Demonstrates how the labour-value form necessitates monetary reserves.

slides.title: Ricardo's Error on Currency and Prices
bullets: Ricardo's false assumption that gold is only coin, and that therefore all imported gold swells the currency, causing prices to rise, while all exported gold reduces the currency leading to a fall of prices, this theoretical assumption is turned into a practical experiment.
Metallic currency has its remedy in the import and export of precious metals which immediately enter circulation.

This error therefore treats every shipment of bullion as an immediate alteration in the money supply, ignoring that gold must first be produced by labour before it can serve as coin or affect prices at all.
The practical result is that policy makers chase symptoms in the sphere of circulation while the real determinants of value, located in the labour process itself, remain untouched and misunderstood.
slides.notes: Critiques a rival view while reaffirming labour-time determination of value.

slides.title: Money Distinguished from Capital
bullets: When gold, or bank notes in its place, circulate in the home trade as means of payment, they constitute capital at the same time.
It is the fact that gold is distinguished from commodities in its capacity of money and not in that of capital, that makes it the means of payment.

Thus the same gold coin settles a merchant’s debt in circulation without entering the labour process where value is expanded, showing that payment remains a finite act of exchange while capital requires the continuous pumping of surplus from living labour.

This functional split therefore exposes a real tension in everyday trade: treating money as if it were already capital conceals the fact that only the wage relation and the labour theory of value turn inert tokens into self-augmenting wealth.
slides.notes: Separates the value form from the capital relation.

slides.title: The Method Begins from Concrete Production Relations
bullets: All production is appropriation of nature by the individual within and through a definite form of society.
The question as to whether wealth grows more rapidly under this or that form of property is not even raised here as yet.

These definite social forms therefore fix in advance how individuals stand to one another and to their instruments, so that any later inquiry into distribution or class already carries the stamp of that original appropriation.

From this concrete ground the analysis can later ask which forms release or fetter the growth of wealth, yet the method itself begins by registering the social character of production rather than measuring its comparative results.
slides.notes: Returns to the material base of production as the starting point of analysis.
